name: Cumbres de la Sierra de Arburu — circular Zona Especial de Conservación de Entzia
region_intro: Hiking in Izki offers access to diverse landscapes, from the dramatic Hajar Mountains to serene wadis and historical settlements. The region is characterized by mountainous terrain, including areas leading up to Jabal Akhdar, and features several wadis like Wadi Halfayn, Wadi Mahram, and Wadi Qurai. Ancient villages such as Harat al Nizar and Harat al Yemen, along with historical forts and Falaj systems, provide cultural points of interest along many routes. This combination of natural features and historical sites makes Izki a unique destination for outdoor exploration. Best bus station hiking trails in Izki The most popular bus station hiking route is Cumbres de la Sierra de Arburu — circular Zona Especial de Conservación de Entzia, a 7.1 miles (11.4 km) trail that takes 3 hours 54 minutes to complete. This moderate route offers views of the surrounding mountainous landscape. Another top favourite among local hikers is Peñas de Obi – Peñalascinco (907m) loop from Arabako Mendialdea, a moderate 7.2 miles (11.6 km) path. This trail winds through varied terrain, including ascents to notable peaks. Local hikers also love the Fajeta Peña Rasgada – Peñalascinco (907m) loop from Arabako Mendialdea, a 4.9 miles (7.9 km) trail leading through mountainous areas, often completed in about 3 hours. Hiking in Izki is defined by mountainous terrain, wadi systems, and ancient villages, offering a blend of natural beauty and historical exploration. The network provides options for different ability levels, from easy walks to more challenging mountain ascents. The routes in Izki are highly rated by the komoot community with an average score of 4.7 stars from more than 360 reviews. More than 2,800 hikers have used komoot to explore Izki's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: How many hiking trails are accessible from bus stations in Izki?
A: There are over 80 hiking routes in Izki that are easily accessible from bus stations. These trails cater to various skill levels, with 13 easy, 56 moderate, and 15 difficult options available.

Q: Are there any easy, family-friendly hikes starting from Izki bus stations?
A: Yes, Izki offers several easy routes suitable for families. A great option is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/10515574" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Vasco-Navarro Railway Bridge – Hermitage of Solitude loop from Arabako Mendialdea&lt;/a&gt;. This easy trail covers approximately 7.4 km with minimal elevation gain, making it perfect for a relaxed outing.

Q: What kind of natural features can I expect to see on hikes around Izki?
A: Izki is nestled in a region with diverse natural beauty. You can explore dramatic mountainous terrain, serene wadis like Wadi Halfayn and Wadi Qurai, and even discover caves such as Jarnan Cave. Hikers often enjoy views from points like the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/2934869" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Izki Gorge Viewpoint&lt;/a&gt; or reaching summits like &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/2714569" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Kapildui Summit&lt;/a&gt; and &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/2934880" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Soila&lt;/a&gt;.

Q: Are there any circular hiking routes available from Izki bus stations?
A: Yes, many routes are circular, allowing you to start and end at the same point, which is convenient when relying on public transport. An example is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1050699654" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Cumbres de la Sierra de Arburu — circular Zona Especial de Conservación de Entzia&lt;/a&gt;, a moderate 11.4 km loop.

Q: What historical or cultural landmarks can I discover while hiking in Izki?
A: Izki is rich in history. You can explore ancient villages like Harat al Nizar and Harat al Yemen, which feature traditional architecture and ancient 'aflaj' irrigation systems. The region also boasts historical forts, towers, and the intriguing Zukait Tombs. You might even spot a &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/3387367" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Castle&lt;/a&gt; during your hike.

Q: What is the best time of year to go hiking in Izki?
A: The best time for hiking in Izki is generally during the cooler months, from autumn to spring (October to April), to avoid the intense summer heat. This period offers more comfortable temperatures for exploring the wadis and mountainous trails.

Q: Are there any longer, more challenging trails accessible by bus?
A: For those seeking a longer challenge, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/38175864" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Hermitage of Solitude – Atauri Viaduct loop from Arabako Mendialdea&lt;/a&gt; is a moderate 17.2 km route with some elevation gain, offering a more extended adventure while still being accessible.

Q: What do other hikers say about the trails in Izki?
A: The hiking trails in Izki are highly regarded by the komoot community, with an average rating of 4.7 out of 5 stars from over 360 reviews. Hikers often praise the diverse landscapes, the historical elements, and the well-maintained paths.

Q: Are dogs allowed on the hiking trails around Izki?
A: While specific regulations can vary by trail and protected areas, many outdoor trails in Izki are generally dog-friendly. It's always recommended to keep dogs on a leash, especially in areas with wildlife or near livestock, and to carry waste bags.

Q: Are there any permits required for hiking in the Izki region?
A: Generally, no specific permits are required for day hiking on most established trails in the Izki region. However, if you plan to venture into specific protected areas or undertake more challenging, multi-day treks, it's advisable to check with local authorities or park management for any specific requirements.

Q: Where can I find facilities or a place to rest after a hike near Izki bus stations?
A: Izki, as a historic town, offers amenities. You can find cafes and local eateries within the town itself, often a short walk from the main bus station. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/6613719" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Izki Park House&lt;/a&gt; might also offer facilities or information for visitors.
tips: Impressive rocky pass, a joy to cross, yes, the height at the lowest point will not exceed 1 meter and you have to walk with a little care (especially if it is wet), but it is well worth the walk that follows the PR bordering the entire fajeta. In "pequeñito" I remember some other belt from Ordesa. super super nice!
1041 scraped meters, but very good views. The mailbox is original, like almost all of these tailpieces.
In the middle of the Santa Agueda and the Mendibibila. The mailbox looks like a chess bishop.
The huge mailbox is like a scale with wheels, simulating bells, and with a hammer to hit them. It's nice to get to "mailboxes" like these. Tremendous views.

No matter how many maps I've looked at (Komoot, Google, IGN, OSM, Garmin, Gaia, Outdoor...) I haven't found another name for this mountain other than "Mailbox". By the way, very curious too, it has two small hammers to touch the four metal sheets.
Again another cool mailbox in the tailpiece, also with car tires. The views are also very cool.
Now converted into a passage for hikers and cyclists, very curious to see and cross.
Beautiful construction and passage of the river, if it were summer they would make you want to bathe.
